name: Misiones
history: Misiones Province emerged as a significant region through the Jesuit missions established in the 17th century, which brought indigenous Guaraní peoples into organized settlements and introduced Christianity and European agricultural practices. After the expulsion of the Jesuits in the 18th century, the region remained sparsely populated until the late 19th and early 20th centuries, when European immigration, particularly from Paraguay, Poland, and Germany, transformed it into an agricultural frontier. The province was formally established in 1881 and developed rapidly through yerba mate cultivation and forestry, becoming one of Argentina's most important agricultural centers. Today, Misiones stands as a unique blend of indigenous Guaraní heritage, colonial Spanish influence, and European immigrant traditions.
culturalNotes: Misiones is deeply characterized by its Guaraní heritage and strong connection to nature, with the lush subtropical landscape serving as the heart of local identity and economic life. The region is renowned for yerba mate production and consumption, with the preparation and sharing of mate being a central social ritual that reflects the fusion of indigenous and European traditions. The province's distinct culture also celebrates its immigrant communities through festivals, cuisine, and architecture, while maintaining deep ecological consciousness tied to the Paraná Forest and Iguazu Falls.
bestTimeToVisit: May to September provides cooler, drier weather for visiting Iguazu Falls and subtropical rainforests.
languageNotes: While Spanish is the official language, Guaraní is widely spoken and understood throughout Misiones, reflecting the region's indigenous roots and cultural pride. Many locals use Guaraní words and phrases in everyday speech, and visitors may encounter bilingual signage and hear the melodic Guaraní language spoken in markets, rural areas, and among older generations.

places.name: Iguazu Falls
places.description: A spectacular UNESCO World Heritage Site featuring 275 waterfalls cascading over the Paraná Plateau with extraordinary power and beauty. The Argentine side offers intimate jungle trails and views of the iconic Devil's Throat waterfall.

activities.detail: Hike the dramatic Garganta del Diablo trail that leads to a viewing platform directly above the most powerful cascade where 14 waterfalls converge in thunderous spray.
Experience the rainforest canopy on suspended bridges and elevated platforms among native trees, birds, and subtropical wildlife without descending to ground level.
Take a speedboat excursion that approaches the base of the falls, getting soaked by mist and spray while experiencing the raw power of cascading water up close.
Walk the accessible trail system through dense jungle that offers multiple viewpoints of different waterfall sections and opportunities to spot toucans, coatis, and butterflies.
Trek the upper-level paths that provide panoramic views across the entire waterfall system and access to quieter vantage points above the main cascade areas.
Visit the falls at night under full moon and artificial lighting to witness the waterfalls illuminated in colors, a mystical perspective unavailable during daylight hours.

food.detail: A savory cornmeal and cheese cake baked until golden, reflecting the Guaraní indigenous heritage and served as a traditional snack throughout Misiones Province.
A refreshing cold herbal infusion made from yerba mate, ice, and medicinal herbs like spearmint, essential to the regional culture and perfect for the subtropical climate.
Grilled freshwater surubí fish caught from the Paraná River, seasoned simply with lemon and served at riverside restaurants near the falls.
A creamy corn soup thickened with cornmeal and enriched with cheese and sometimes meat, a comfort dish rooted in Guaraní culinary traditions.
Participate in the social ritual of sharing a gourd of hot yerba mate passed among friends, a fundamental custom of Argentine culture practiced throughout the region.

culture.detail: Keep at least 2 meters from coatis, toucans, and other fauna despite their apparent friendliness; feeding animals habituates them dangerously and is strictly prohibited.
While English is spoken in tourist areas, locals appreciate visitors attempting basic Spanish phrases like 'por favor' and 'gracias' as a sign of respect for Argentine culture.
Arrive at the falls before 9 AM to avoid peak tourist crowds and enjoy clearer skies, better wildlife visibility, and more serene jungle atmosphere.
The falls and region hold spiritual significance for the indigenous Guaraní people; approach the site with reverence and consider visiting the Guaraní craft markets to support local artisans.

places.name: Oberá
places.description: The cultural heart of Misiones province known as the City of Orchids, featuring vibrant markets, local crafts, and authentic Misionero culture. It serves as a good base for exploring the central region and experiencing provincial life away from major tourist routes.

activities.detail: Visit Oberá's signature orchid park showcasing hundreds of native and cultivated orchid species in a lush botanical setting.
Browse the craft market in the city center where local artisans sell handmade woodwork, ceramics, and traditional Misionero textiles.
Explore the main plaza featuring colonial architecture, local shops, and a weekly farmers market showcasing regional produce and yerba mate.
Walk the nature trail on the outskirts of the city through subtropical forest with opportunities to spot native birds and plant species.
Learn about Oberá's history and Misionero culture through exhibits on indigenous heritage, yerba mate production, and regional development.
Visit working farms around Oberá to see yerba mate cultivation, tobacco farming, and learn traditional agricultural practices of the region.

food.detail: A cheesy cornbread made with cassava flour that is a ubiquitous breakfast staple and street snack throughout Misiones.
Cold yerba mate drink served with medicinal herbs and ice, essential to local social culture and refreshing in the subtropical climate.
Fried pastries filled with ground meat, corn, or cheese, representing the region's culinary fusion of Spanish and Guaraní influences.
A thick cornmeal and cheese dish that despite its name is more of a savory cake, common in the tri-border region of Misiones.
Purchase freshly harvested and processed yerba mate directly from local producers or markets to experience the highest quality from Argentina's primary growing region.

culture.detail: Accept mate when offered and pass it back to the host after drinking—sharing mate is a fundamental social gesture and refusing is considered impolite.
Locals speak Spanish with many Guaraní words mixed in; learning basic greetings and terms like 'che' (my) shows respect for the region's indigenous heritage.
Oberá operates on a slower schedule than major cities; many shops close for afternoon siesta and business moves at a leisurely pace reflecting provincial life.
The community takes pride in sustainable farming and local craftsmanship, so purchasing directly from artisans and farmers supports the local economy and culture.

places.name: San Ignacio
places.description: Home to one of South America's most important Jesuit mission ruins dating to the 17th century, a UNESCO World Heritage Site showcasing remarkable stone architecture. The site offers a fascinating glimpse into colonial religious history and indigenous culture.

activities.detail: Walk through the remarkably preserved 17th-century Jesuit mission complex featuring original stone structures, church foundations, and residences that reveal colonial religious and indigenous life.

activities.text: Attend the Sound and Light Show
activities.detail: Experience an evening multimedia presentation that illuminates the mission ruins with projections and narration telling the history of the Jesuit missions and Guaraní people.
View artifacts, sculptures, and explanatory exhibits housed within the mission grounds that contextualize the archaeological significance and daily life of the former settlement.
Trek through subtropical Misiones forest surrounding the ruins to observe native flora and fauna while discovering additional mission-related archaeological sites.
Travel 40 kilometers north to see another significant Jesuit mission ruin, allowing comparison between different mission architectural styles and settlement layouts.
Take a short drive to experience the nearby Iguazú Falls, one of the world's most spectacular natural wonders, combining cultural and natural attractions in one trip.

food.detail: A warm, cheesy cassava flour bread that is a traditional Guaraní staple and beloved breakfast item throughout Misiones, often enjoyed with tereré or coffee.
A refreshing cold mate-based herbal drink mixed with medicinal and aromatic yerba, water, and ice that is the quintessential social beverage of the region.
Grilled river catfish caught from the Paraná, prepared simply with local seasonings and served as a local specialty showcasing the region's freshwater gastronomy.
Crispy fried cassava root served as a popular side dish or street food, reflecting the indigenous agricultural heritage and Guaraní food traditions.
A traditional cornmeal pancake filled with cheese that represents authentic Guaraní cuisine and is commonly served for breakfast or light meals in local restaurants.

culture.detail: The mission ruins remain spiritually significant to local Guaraní communities; visitors should dress modestly, speak quietly, and avoid disruptive behavior while exploring.
While some guides speak English, many locals and vendors in San Ignacio primarily speak Spanish and Guaraní, so basic Spanish phrases will enhance your experience and interactions.
Many local shops and services close during early afternoon hours; plan your activities accordingly and take advantage of the slower pace to rest during the hottest part of the day.
Accepting an offered gourd of tereré or hot mate from locals is a sign of friendship and cultural respect; take small sips and pass it back with gratitude.

places.name: Cristo Rei
places.description: A 14-meter tall statue of Christ overlooking Puerto Iguazú with panoramic views of three countries where Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay meet. It provides orientation, spiritual significance, and stunning viewpoints of the region.

activities.detail: Capture wide-angle shots of the tri-border region where the Paraná and Iguazú rivers converge, with views spanning Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay from the statue's elevated platform.
Arrive early morning to photograph the statue silhouetted against the sunrise over the Paraná River and witness the mist rising from the Iguazú Falls in the distance.
Spend quiet time at the base of the 14-meter Christ statue, which serves as a pilgrimage site for Catholic visitors seeking spiritual contemplation overlooking three nations.

activities.text: Iguazú Falls Day Trip from Cristo Rei
activities.detail: Use Cristo Rei as an orientation point before heading to nearby Iguazú National Park, located approximately 17 kilometers away, to experience one of the world's largest waterfall systems.
Walk through Puerto Iguazú's nearby markets to observe the cultural blend of Argentine, Brazilian, and Paraguayan merchants selling regional crafts and goods within sight of the statue.
Return to the Cristo Rei viewpoint at dusk to watch the sun set over the Paraná River while the three-country border becomes illuminated in evening light.

food.detail: A warm, corn-based Paraguayan bread often sold by street vendors near Cristo Rei that is savory, slightly sweet, and culturally significant to the tri-border region.
A refreshing cold herbal beverage made from yerba mate, ice, and medicinal herbs that locals drink throughout the day, especially popular in Puerto Iguazú's subtropical heat.
Grilled surubí fish, a large river fish native to the Paraná River, served with lime and fresh vegetables at local restaurants near the Cristo Rei area.
Misiones province specializes in empanadas filled with beef, chicken, or spinach, representing the blend of Spanish and indigenous Guaraní culinary traditions.
Sample locally-made dulce de leche in Puerto Iguazú shops, often made with different infusions like yerba mate or tropical fruit flavors unique to the Misiones region.

culture.detail: Wear modest, covered clothing when visiting Cristo Rei, as it is an active spiritual pilgrimage site for Catholic devotees and local residents.
Spanish, Portuguese, and Guaraní are spoken in Puerto Iguazú; learning a few basic phrases in each language enhances interactions with locals near the tri-border monument.
Cristo Rei draws larger crowds during Argentine holidays and religious celebrations; visit on quieter weekdays to experience a more intimate spiritual atmosphere.
While at Cristo Rei you can see the borders of three countries, but crossing requires proper documentation; use Argentine pesos in Puerto Iguazú and respect the tri-border security presence.

places.name: Los Saltos de Moconá
places.description: A series of waterfalls and rapids within Moconá National Park where the Uruguay River drops dramatically through spectacular gorges. It represents one of Argentina's most pristine and least-visited natural attractions for intrepid explorers.

activities.detail: Navigate by motorboat through the dramatic Uruguay River gorges to witness the waterfalls and rapids from water level, the primary way to experience Los Saltos de Moconá.
Trek along marked paths through subtropical forest adjacent to the river to access viewpoints overlooking the cascades and observe local wildlife.
Position yourself at key vantage points early morning when mist rises from the falls and light filters through the gorge, ideal for capturing the landscape.
Search for toucans, jaguars, coatis, and river birds in the pristine subtropical forest surrounding the national park with a trained guide.
Stay overnight at designated campsites within Moconá National Park to experience the soundscape of the falls and forest at night.
Paddle kayaks in designated safer areas of the Uruguay River adjacent to the gorge, weather and water conditions permitting.

food.detail: A local river fish common in the Paraná region, grilled simply with lemon and served at lodges and small restaurants near the park entrance.
A traditional Misiones corn and cheese bread baked fresh by local vendors and sold at the park visitor areas as a portable snack.
The ubiquitous social beverage of northern Argentina, shared communally by guides and park staff as a welcoming tradition during excursions.
Savory pastries filled with meat, cheese, or vegetables reflecting regional Misiones cooking, available at nearby towns like Oberá.
Local papayas, passion fruit, and bananas sold at roadside stands and park facilities, often served fresh or as juices at small cafés.

culture.detail: Los Saltos de Moconá is remote and boat tours require licensed operators; arrange excursions through your lodge or park office at least one day ahead.
The park is a protected reserve; guides enforce strict rules against approaching animals or removing plants, essential for conservation of this pristine ecosystem.
Subtropical humidity and forest environment mean mosquitoes and other insects are prevalent; local residents routinely use repellent and recommend long sleeves at dusk.
Few park staff and local guides speak English; basic Spanish phrases and a translation app are invaluable for communication and safety.

places.name: Wanda
places.description: A small mining town famous for its semi-precious stone mines including amethyst, topaz, and tourmaline that visitors can explore and purchase directly. It offers a unique glimpse into regional geology and local artisanal mining traditions.

activities.detail: Descend into working amethyst mines to see veins of purple crystal in their natural setting and learn traditional extraction methods from local miners.
Pan for and extract colorful topaz and tourmaline specimens in designated mining areas, keeping what you find as souvenirs.
Visit artisanal stonecutting studios where craftspeople polish and shape raw gems into jewelry and decorative pieces.
Browse numerous small shops selling uncut and polished semi-precious stones directly from local miners at competitive prices.
Explore exhibits displaying specimens of amethyst, topaz, tourmaline and other minerals from the region with explanations of local geology.
Visit family-run mining operations in the surrounding countryside to understand multigenerational artisanal extraction practices.

food.detail: A savory corn cake baked with cheese and corn kernels, a traditional Misiones dish that reflects indigenous Guaraní influences.
Participate in the shared drinking of hot mate, a caffeine-rich herbal infusion that is central to regional social customs.
Sample locally-made blood sausage and chorizo from regional producers, often grilled and served at family gatherings.
Crispy-fried cassava root cut into sticks, a simple carbohydrate staple served with most meals in the region.
A hearty cornmeal and cheese soup that is a staple comfort food shared across the tri-border region of Misiones.

culture.detail: Prices for raw stones are expected to be negotiated; polite haggling over gem purchases is standard practice and welcomed by vendors.
These are multigenerational mining operations; approach miners and their workspace with genuine curiosity and respect for their dangerous, skilled work.
English is rarely spoken in Wanda; learning basic Spanish phrases will greatly enhance interactions with local shopkeepers and miners.
Most small vendors and miners operate on a cash-only basis, so bring Argentine pesos rather than relying on cards.

places.name: Eldorado
places.description: The second-largest city in Misiones province historically significant for mate and timber industries, offering authentic local culture and regional products. It serves as a commercial hub and base for exploring central and southern Misiones attractions.

activities.text: Mate Museum (Museo de la Yerba Mate)
activities.detail: Learn about the history and production of yerba mate, a central part of Misiones culture, with exhibits on traditional processing and regional varieties.
Visit local sawmills and timber yards to understand Eldorado's economic foundation and see how the region processes its abundant forest resources.
Browse local produce, fresh mate, handicrafts, and regional products in the bustling commercial heart of the city.
Explore waterfalls and lush forest trails about 20 kilometers from Eldorado, offering swimming and nature walks in pristine subtropical terrain.
Visit this unique wooden structure built in the traditional Guaraní style, located near Eldorado, showcasing indigenous architectural heritage.
Visit workshops where artisans craft wooden furniture, yerba mate gourds (mates), and other handmade items using regional materials.

food.detail: Experience the local tradition of drinking ice-cold tereré, a refreshing yerba mate preparation with medicinal herbs, especially popular in hot afternoons.
Taste this traditional Misiones cheese bread made with cornmeal, cheese, and eggs, often enjoyed at breakfast or as a snack.
Sample this cassava and cheese pancake, a staple regional dish that reflects Guaraní culinary influence throughout Misiones.
Buy locally grown passion fruit, guava, and papaya from street vendors and markets, harvested fresh from regional orchards.
Enjoy fresh dorado fish grilled over an open flame at local restaurants, a specialty from the Paraná River region nearby.

culture.detail: When offered mate, accept with your right hand, drink through the shared metal straw (bombilla), and return the gourd to your host—refusing is considered rude.
Many locals speak a blend of Spanish and Guaraní; learning basic Guaraní phrases like 'Mba'eichapa' (how are you) shows respect for regional heritage.
Markets and businesses often close for a long midday break (roughly 12–3 PM) and operate into evening; plan shopping accordingly.
Pack lightweight clothing, sunscreen, and insect repellent year-round; locals dress lightly and take siestas during peak afternoon heat.

places.name: Puerto Bemberg
places.description: A historic port town on the Paraná River with colonial architecture and access to the river's scenic beauty and local Misionero traditions. It provides a quieter alternative to tourist-heavy destinations while offering river-based activities and cultural experiences.

activities.detail: Take guided motorboat excursions along the Paraná River to observe local wildlife, including toucans and caimans, while learning about the region's ecological significance.
Explore the town's historic buildings and waterfront district featuring early 20th-century structures that reflect Puerto Bemberg's importance as a former timber export hub.
Visit nearby estancias growing yerba mate to see traditional harvesting methods and participate in the preparation of this central Misionero beverage.
Browse local crafts, woodwork, and handmade textiles from regional artisans at the town's market, typically held on weekends near the waterfront.
Arrange picnics along the Paraná's banks at dusk with local provisions, experiencing the river's tranquil beauty and indigenous-influenced landscape.

food.detail: A cold yerba mate drink traditionally served in a gourd and sipped communally through a metal straw, often enjoyed in riverside settings with fresh mint and local water.
A cheese-filled cornmeal bread baked fresh daily and sold by local vendors, representing traditional Misionero and Guaraní-influenced cuisine.
Grilled surubí catfish caught from the Paraná River, typically seasoned simply with citrus and served at riverside restaurants with fresh vegetables.
A creamed corn dish made with local corn varieties, often prepared with cheese and served as a side at family-run establishments throughout town.
Golden fried pastries traditionally eaten for breakfast or afternoon tea, often served warm with honey or dulce de leche at local cafés.

culture.detail: Plan activities around the Paraná's water levels and local ferry schedules, as seasonal variations significantly affect boat access and river-based transportation.
When offered tererê or mate, accept graciously and learn to pass the gourd counterclockwise; refusing is considered impolite in social settings.
Time your visit around local celebrations honoring patron saints or harvest festivals, when colonial plazas host traditional music, dance, and regional food stalls.
English is rarely spoken in Puerto Bemberg; learning basic Spanish or Portuguese phrases enhances interactions with locals and demonstrates cultural respect.

places.name: Aristóbulo del Valle
places.description: A charming rural town in the heart of Misiones known for traditional crafts, local agriculture, and authentic provincial atmosphere. It offers travelers an immersive experience of everyday Misionero life away from commercialized tourist centers.

activities.detail: Tour artisan studios where Misionero craftspeople create traditional wooden furniture, ceramics, and woven textiles using generations-old techniques.
Walk through nearby yerba mate fields and learn about cultivation and harvesting from local farmers who have worked the land for decades.
Browse the town plaza market on weekends where vendors sell fresh produce, handmade goods, and regional products directly from local producers.
Trek through surrounding subtropical forest to observe native flora and fauna while experiencing the lush Misionero landscape.
Arrange visits to working family farms producing tobacco, cassava, or tropical fruits and participate in daily agricultural activities.
Visit the modest town church and colonial-era buildings that reflect Aristóbulo del Valle's heritage and connection to regional settlement patterns.

food.detail: A savory corn and cheese cake baked fresh in local kitchens, representing authentic Misionero comfort food often served at family gatherings.
The local cold yerba mate beverage prepared with medicinal jungle herbs and ice, served communally as a social ritual throughout the day.
Crispy fried cassava root served as a simple side dish at rural restaurants and farm stands, staple of provincial Misiones cuisine.
Hearty broth made from free-range local chicken simmered with root vegetables and served in modest family-run comedores.
A creamed corn dish with cheese and onions representing traditional Guaraní-influenced cooking still prepared in home kitchens throughout town.

culture.detail: Most local businesses close between noon and 4 PM; plan activities accordingly and avoid scheduling important visits during traditional rest time.
When offered tereré or mate in social settings, accept graciously and drink from the shared gourd using the metal straw, passing it back to the host.
While some residents speak Guaraní, Spanish is essential for communication; English is rarely spoken so basic phrases are invaluable for meaningful interaction.
Purchase crafts and goods directly from makers in their workshops rather than middlemen to sustain traditional livelihoods and authentic experiences.

places.name: Salto Encantado
places.description: A beautiful waterfall and natural pool in a forested setting popular for swimming and nature walks in the northeastern region of Misiones. It offers a refreshing break from the rainforest with accessible trails and scenic picnic areas.

activities.detail: Wade and swim in the crystal-clear emerald pool at the base of the waterfall, fed by the cascade and surrounded by lush rainforest.
Capture stunning shots of the main 20-meter waterfall framed by dense vegetation and mist, best in morning light.
Follow the well-maintained trails through the Atlantic Forest canopy spotting native birds, butterflies, and subtropical flora.
Enjoy packed lunches at shaded picnic grounds with tables and facilities overlooking the waterfall and forest.
Identify toucans, parrots, tanagers, and other Misiones native species in their natural rainforest habitat along the trails.
Hike the short, easy path down to the pool's edge for an immersive experience beneath the cascading water.

food.detail: A warm, cheesy cornbread ring typical of Misiones, often sold by vendors near the park entrance.
A refreshing cold herbal drink made with yerba mate and local herbs, perfect for cooling off after hiking.
Grilled or pan-fried river trout caught locally, often available at nearby lodges and restaurants.
Stuffed pastries filled with meat, cheese, or vegetables using regional ingredients and cooking methods.

food.text: Dulce de Leche with Local Fruit
food.detail: Sweet caramel-like paste served with fresh tropical fruits grown in Misiones' rich soil.

culture.detail: Stay on marked trails to protect delicate vegetation and wildlife habitats; the forest is sacred to the Guaraní heritage of the region.
Mosquitoes and other insects are common year-round in the humid subtropical climate, so come prepared with DEET-based protection.
Plan your visit May through September when water levels are stable and trails are safest; summer months (November-March) bring heavy rainfall.
Most local guides and vendors speak limited English, so knowing simple phrases enhances your interaction and appreciation of local culture.

restaurants.name: La Cantera
restaurants.description: Premier steakhouse near Iguazu Falls with impeccable beef cuts and extensive wine selection.

restaurants.name: El Quincho del Tío Querido
restaurants.description: Rustic riverside grill specializing in fresh surubí and traditional asado with casual atmosphere.
restaurants.mustOrder: Surubí a la manteca (butter-poached river fish)

restaurants.name: Aqaba Resto Bar
restaurants.description: Contemporary fusion restaurant with creative dishes blending local ingredients and global techniques.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Garupá
restaurants.description: Family-run establishment serving traditional Misiones cuisine with regional specialties and friendly service.

restaurants.name: Lo de Rafa
restaurants.description: Popular local grill joint known for quality meat, reasonable prices, and lively afternoon crowds.
restaurants.mustOrder: Asado de tira with grilled vegetables

restaurants.name: La Casa de la Pasta
restaurants.description: Homemade pasta shop with traditional recipes and daily specials reflecting Italian-Argentine fusion.
restaurants.mustOrder: Ravioles de carne con salsa casera

restaurants.name: Restaurante Puerto Yerba
restaurants.description: Countryside restaurant featuring yerba mate culture and traditional local dishes in serene garden setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Tereré with local cheese and sausage

restaurants.name: Fonda Tía María
restaurants.description: Authentic family-style restaurant offering hearty portions of traditional recipes at budget-friendly prices.

restaurants.name: Chacra Hotel Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale hotel restaurant combining local produce with refined techniques in elegant setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Medallones de cerdo with chimichurri sauce

restaurants.name: Restaurante Casa Blanca
restaurants.description: Elegant downtown steakhouse with river views, premium beef selection, and refined wine pairings.
restaurants.mustOrder: Carne a la salsa de hongos (beef in mushroom sauce)

restaurants.name: La Esquina de Don Carlos
restaurants.description: Corner grill institution serving classic Argentine fare with emphasis on traditional preparation methods.

restaurants.name: Resto Boutique Karaí
restaurants.description: Intimate restaurant showcasing modern interpretations of regional Misiones ingredients and flavors.
restaurants.mustOrder: Surubí with passion fruit and local herb sauce

restaurants.name: Cantina de Don Juan
restaurants.description: Rural cantina preserving traditional Misiones food culture with authentic recipes and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Bella Italia
restaurants.description: Family pizzeria and pasta house near Wanda's famous gemstone mines with wood-fired oven pizzas.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pizza a la piedra with local ingredients

restaurants.name: Moconá Lodge Restaurant
restaurants.address: Parque Provincial Moconá access road, Moconá
restaurants.description: Exclusive lodge restaurant in remote national park setting featuring foraged ingredients and local specialties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Seven-course tasting menu with Paraná river fish
